title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Spring 2026 | Tuesdays 2:00-3:30 PM | Education 2 South L28-2206
February 17 – May 5, 2026

title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Course description
Rethinking data analysis is an advanced short course designed to:

Discuss common misunderstandings & typical errors in the practice of data analysis.
Provide a mental toolkit for critically thinking about analysis methods and results.

Classes will involve lectures, discussions, hands-on exercises, and homework about concepts critical to the day-to-day use and consumption of quantitative/computational techniques for working with data.
Note: Open to graduate students for credit. Trainees and professionals at all stages are welcome to unofficially audit this course.

Prerequisites

Introductory knowledge of statistics and probability, and
Introductory experience with data wrangling, analysis, and visualization using R or Python. Pre-written R & Python code will be provided and learners will be expected to understand, modify, and run the code. These are strict requirements for students taking the course for credit and a strong recommendation for those unofficially auditing it.

The course website will contain all the information related to this course including pointers to free online preparatory materials.

**NO CLASS; Watch video** | AI-assisted data analysis
Prep • Video • Slides • Readings







Weekly topics overview

Week 1: Scientific method and hypothesis formation • Recognizing when study designs fail • Critical evaluation of research claims • Cognitive biases in data interpretation • Statistical intuition vs. blind rule-following
Week 2: Uncertainty laundering • Confidence intervals vs. prediction intervals • Bootstrap methods • Error propagation • How missing data gets mishandled • The broken ritual of normality testing
Week 3: What P-values measure (and what they don’t) • P-hacking: subtle and obvious forms • Multiple testing problem • Publication bias and file drawer problem • Conflicts of interest and analytical flexibility
Week 4: Type I and Type II errors in practice • Statistical power: the underpowered majority • The “Sizeless Stare”: statistical vs. practical significance • Sample size planning: beyond ritual
Week 5: Pseudoreplication: getting the experimental unit wrong • Effective sample size • Confounding variables • Batch effects and technical artifacts
Week 6: Circular analysis: dipping into the data twice • Regression to the mean • Sampling biases: who’s missing and why it matters • Pre-registration: the solution to many problems
Week 7: Bayesian vs. frequentist: different questions • Base rate neglect • Prior specification • Communicating probabilistic results
Week 8: Correlation vs. causation • Spurious correlations • Effect modification and interaction • Model misspecification • Appropriate measures for different data types
Week 9: Curse of dimensionality • Overfitting and model selection • Effective evaluation strategies • Feature selection and multiple testing • Privacy risks in high-dimensional data
Week 10: Principles of effective data visualization • Common visualization mistakes and deceptions • Communicating uncertainty visually • Audience-appropriate statistical communication
Week 11: Data management and documentation • Code organization and version control • Reproducible analysis workflows • Collaborative research practices • Responsible data sharing practices • Ethical transparency through documentation
Week 12: The promise and peril of AI for data analysis • AI failure modes in statistical analysis • Using AI responsibly and effectively • Building AI literacy for data analysis • Ethical dimensions of AI-assisted analysis

title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Communication and office hours

Slack workspace
Slack will be the primary mode of communication for this course, including major announcements. All students should have received invitations to join the course Slack workspace at their CU email address.
Important emails: Though the bulk of communication will take place via Slack, important course information will occasionally be sent via email to your CU email address (@cuanschutz.edu). You are responsible for all information sent to your University email account and for checking this account regularly.


Office hours
Instructor office hours: TBD
Location: AHSB 7082 (or via Zoom)
Notes about office hours: 1. Many questions can be resolved quickly via Slack message—feel free to reach out there first. 2. If you’d like to meet but cannot make the scheduled time, send me a Slack message and we’ll find a time that works for both of us.

title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Course activities and grading

Assignments (40%)
For each topic, you will be assigned reading materials and, occasionally, a coding assignment. Links to these materials will be posted on the course website next to each topic in the Calendar, with instructions provided on Slack.
Typically, each completed assignment is due before the next class.


Class participation (40%)
Active participation is essential to your learning in this course. This includes:

Completing assignments and additional readings
Attending class regularly and on time
Working in groups during in-class discussion sessions
Asking questions about statistical or programming concepts (no one has the perfect background!)
Contributing to materials in-class and on Slack
Correcting me when I am wrong



Final exam (20%)
Details will be provided later in the semester.

title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Course policies

Class presence
First and foremost: If you sincerely engage with the class and put in earnest effort, you will do well.

Creating a psychologically safe environment
For learning and betterment to occur, I need everyone’s contribution to ensure that this class (and all its affiliated spaces including Slack) remain a psychologically safe environment where:

If learners make a mistake, others (me included) will not penalize or think less of them for it, and
Others will not resent or humiliate them when they ask for help or information.

I expect everyone to respect and trust each other so that every learner is confident that the group won’t embarrass, reject, or punish someone for speaking up. To sustain this environment, all of us will listen, stay curious, and be honest while staying away from judgment, unsolicited advice-giving, and interrupting.


Attendance policy
Consistent class presence and participation is required to do well in this course, as most activities are based on material presented and worked on in class.
If you have a legitimate reason to miss class (job/graduate school/medical school interviews, health/medical needs, or any other life circumstances), please let me know as soon as you can. I will always be willing to work with you to figure out how to accommodate your constraints and how you may make up for any lost time/points if needed. The same goes for meeting assignment deadlines—if you need more time because of extenuating circumstances, keep me posted and I will support you.
Multiple absences without notice or explanation will result in a reduction of points allocated for attendance. Similarly, not completing assignments (or any other milestones) without notice/explanation will result in reduction of points at the instructor’s discretion.



Code of conduct
In addition to our campus’s official code of conduct, I expect all conduct to serve the singular goal of sustaining a friendly, respectful, supportive, and fun environment where we can do our best work and have a great time doing it.
Expected behaviors: - Do work that you’re proud of throughout the course - Be supportive of your fellow learners; respect each other’s strengths, weaknesses, and differences - Communicate openly & respectfully with everyone in the class - When you ask for help from fellow learners, respect and appreciate their time and effort
Classroom etiquette: Respectful and responsible behavior is expected at all times, which includes: - Turning your cell phone off - Refraining from non-course-related use of electronic devices - Not interrupting other students - Not using offensive or demeaning language in our discussions
Flagrant or repeated violations of these expectations may result in ejection from the classroom/session, grade-related penalties, and/or involvement of the university Ombudsperson.

Harassment and discrimination
I am unequivocally dedicated to providing a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of gender, gender identity and expression, age, sexual orientation, disability, physical appearance, body size, race, or religion (or lack thereof). I will not tolerate harassment of colleagues in any form. Behaviors that could be considered discriminatory or harassing, or unwanted sexual attention, will not be tolerated and will be immediately reported to the appropriate CU office (which may include the CU Police Department).


Discussing or raising concerns
If you experience, observe, or just want to talk through anything regarding your or anyone else’s safety and well-being, talk to me. I will not tolerate any discrimination or harassment. You can rest assured that you can come to me and I will listen and I will believe. I will support you and advocate for you with everything within my power.
Note: As a member of the university community, I have the responsibility to report any instances of sexual harassment, sexual violence and/or other forms of prohibited discrimination. If you would rather share information about sexual harassment, sexual violence or discrimination to a confidential employee who does not have this reporting responsibility, I recommend the Ombuds Office that offers students a confidential and impartial place to discuss academic and nonacademic concerns.


Chosen name and pronouns
All people have the right to be addressed and referred to in accordance with their personal identity. Many people do not identify with the name on their birth certificate, school ID, or other forms of identification. At the beginning of this class, everyone indicates the name they use and their pronouns.
If you would like to change your name and/or gender marker, you can do that through the Registrar (Student Resources). Please advise me at any point if you need to update your name and/or pronouns in my records.



Mental and physical health
University life, graduate studies, research, living away from home, and personal relationships—any of these can spring formidable challenges to our mental well-being in unsuspecting, surprising ways.
To deal with these circumstances, I hope you are able to make time to take care of yourself (socialize remotely, exercise, eat healthy, sleep, blow off steam, etc.). I also hope that when you feel low, when your physical/mental wellness is being put to test, you will talk to a friend, a friendly colleague, or a close family member.
I also welcome you to talk to me. You can come to me anytime with any challenge you face and I will listen. I will do my best to learn about any issue you have (without any judgment, I promise), keep anything you share confidential, and offer my thoughts based on my experience. It is my wish, duty, and privilege to be of help in any way to ensure your mental (& physical) well-being so that you can get a great education and have a good time doing it.
Finally, I urge you to take advantage of many resources available on campus to support your health and wellness including the Mental Health Resources at CU.


Academic honesty
The Office of Research Education (ORE) provides an Honor Code policy that we will follow. Here’s a key excerpt:

All ORE PhD programs expect students to adhere to the highest standards of academic honesty and integrity, including completion of individual work as assigned, adherence to department/program requirements and standards and accurate documentation of sources of information and records in classroom and/or laboratory environments. Examples of behavior that violates these standards include academic dishonesty or complicity in such dishonesty, plagiarism (including the undocumented use of internet and web-based information, including GenAI tools like ChatGPT), cheating, illegitimate possession and/or use of examinations, violation of the ethical standards for conducting research, and fabrication or falsification of data and/or official records.

Important note on plagiarism: In the context of this course, plagiarism includes, but is not limited to: - Directly copying another student’s solutions to in-class or homework problems - Copying materials from online sources, textbooks, or other reference materials without citing those references in your source code or documentation - Having somebody else—or something else like GenAI tools including ChatGPT—do your pre-class work, in-class work, or homework on your behalf
Any work that is done in collaboration with other students (or GenAI tools) should state this explicitly, and have their names as well as yours listed clearly.
More broadly, you should strive to uphold values of the highest ethical standard.


Accommodations
If you have a university-documented learning difficulty or require other accommodations, please provide me with your approval as soon as possible and speak with me about how I can assist you in your learning. If you do not have an approval but have been documented with a learning difficulty or other problems for which you may still require accommodation, please contact CU’s Office of Disability, Access and Inclusion (ODAI) to acquire current documentation.

title: HMGP 7622: Rethinking Data Analysis
text:
Frequently asked questions

Why was this course developed?
As many parts of biological and biomedical research are becoming data-intensive, typical graduate training involves courses in coding and statistics. However, students face a significant challenge when trying to apply these learnings to novel research questions and complex datasets. This requires knowing:

What the practical definitions of various concepts and their relationships are
When and why they are applied in certain situations and not others
What is a robust sequence of actions to take when applying them to data
How to judiciously interpret the results
How to clearly and transparently communicate the findings

Currently, most students learn these ideas only by piecing together a mental model of “acceptable/standard/best practices” based on bits of information from mentors, peers, and published papers.
This course addresses this challenge by focusing on common misunderstandings and typical errors in statistical data analysis practice, providing a mental toolkit for critical thinking about statistical methods and results.
Will this course teach me concepts in statistics?
There are already plenty of existing courses at CU that teach introductory, intermediate, and advanced statistics. Search the Academic Catalog using “statistics” under Keyword Search to get the full list. Statistics at the level of BMSC 7820 or BIOS 6601 is strongly recommended before taking this course.
This is a non-traditional course aimed at discussing what happens—issues that crop up and nuances that become relevant—when ideas from traditional courses are applied to actual research, messy data, and real-world problems.
Why is coding (in languages like R or Python) a prerequisite for this course?
The goal is to create opportunities for developing strong intuition behind many concepts in data analysis. This course takes the approach of using programming to implement statistical ideas on real/simulated data and observe the results to build intuition behind various concepts.
If you would like introductory courses in programming (in R or Python) and statistical analysis, please check out BSBT 6805, MOLB 7900/7910, or BIOS 6642/6682.
What are some specific coding skills I would need for this course?
You will be writing code to: - Read in datasets and wrangle them into a convenient format - Call common statistical functions from standard packages/libraries - Implement simulations/tests (random number generation, for/while loops) - Make plots (scatterplot, histograms, boxplots, etc.)
For R users: - tidyverse (readr, dplyr, ggplot2) - Calculating summary statistics (mean/median, std-deviation/variance, correlation) - Generating random numbers (runif, rnorm) - Writing for & while loops
For Python users: - pandas (data wrangling) - seaborn (data visualization) - numpy (summary statistics, random numbers) - Writing for & while loops
You can find recommended free online resources for learning these skills on the course resources page.
I’m a postdoc (or research staff) and would like to just attend the class—no need for credits. Is that possible?
Yes! I’m open to having postdocs, research/staff scientists, and faculty members audit this course for free. The only things I ask in return are your active engagement in the class and course materials along with providing constructive feedback.
Are there going to be exams?
Yes! There will be an end-of-term exam.

title: Software setup guide for statistics course
text:
Choose either R or Python. Both are fully supported.






Download and install R from: https://cran.r-project.org
Choose your operating system and follow installation instructions.





Download RStudio Desktop (free version): https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
Install after R is installed.

RStudio provides a user-friendly interface for coding, plotting, and managing files.




Open RStudio and run:
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "data.table"))
Packages used in this course:

tidyverse → data wrangling (dplyr), plotting (ggplot2), reading data (readr)
data.table → fast data manipulation (optional but useful)

Base R already includes:

mean(), sd(), quantile(), cor()
rnorm(), runif(), sample()
for / while loops
hist(), plot(), boxplot()





Run:
library(tidyverse)

x <- rnorm(100)
mean(x)
hist(x)
If this runs without error and produces a histogram, you’re ready.







Download Anaconda (Individual Edition) from: https://www.anaconda.com/products/distribution
This installs:

Python
Jupyter Notebook
Core scientific libraries

Follow installation instructions for your OS.




Open Anaconda Prompt (or terminal) and run:
conda create -n stats_course python=3.11
conda activate stats_course
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scipy jupyter




We will use:

numpy → arrays, random numbers, simulations
pandas → data wrangling
matplotlib → plotting
seaborn → statistical visualization
scipy → statistical tests

Python built-ins already support:

for / while loops
basic math
random via numpy.random





Start Jupyter:
jupyter notebook
In a new notebook, run:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.random.normal(size=100)
np.mean(x)

plt.hist(x)
plt.show()
If this runs and shows a histogram, you’re ready.

title: Correlation coefficients | Week 08 In-class assignment
text:
Correlation coefficients | Week 08 In-class assignment
This assignment is designed to help you understand what makes a correlation coefficient “statistically significant,” and how much a Pearson r can vary purely by chance when no true relationship exists.
This prewritten script illustrates one core approach:

Part 1 — Simulate the null distribution of Pearson r at a fixed sample size of n = 10: draw 10,000 pairs of completely independent variables, compute r for each, and identify the 95th-percentile critical value. Then examine how wide this distribution is, and what it means for interpreting a “significant” correlation in a small study.

Here’s what to do:

Download the R script or Python script
Load it into your favorite IDE
Work with a partner to go through the code step by step, discuss the logic in detail, and add detailed comments along the way
Run the code and discuss the output and the questions embedded at the end

title: Scientific method, Critical evaluation, and Cognitive biases | Week 01 Assignment
text:
This assignment is designed to help you recognize cognitive biases, evaluate statistical claims, and identify common pitfalls in study design.
Write your answers in a separate text docuent, convert to PDF, and submit using this form.


Task: Read the following descriptions and classify them as “Exploratory” or “Confirmatory” along with a brief (<50-word) reasoning.

Scenario A: A researcher analyzes a large dataset of dietary habits and finds that people who eat more blueberries have a lower risk of stroke; they then publish these results as a primary finding.
Scenario B: A scientist publishes a time-stamped plan on the Open Science Framework (OSF) detailing the exact primary endpoint and statistical model they will use for a drug trial before any patients are recruited.
Scenario C: An investigator explores various ways to transform data (e.g., log-transformations, squaring variables) until a p-value falls below 0.05.




Task: Identify the “Red Flags” in the following research summaries along with a brief (<50-word) reasoning.

Abstract 1: “A study of 12 women showed that a new herbal supplement improved digestion parameters by 30%. No side effects were reported, and we conclude this is an effective GI issue treatment.”
Abstract 2: “Our prospective cohort study found that daily meditation leads to a 15% reduction in heart disease. We recommend mandatory meditation programs in workplaces.”




Task: Match the behavior to the correct fallacy or bias along with a brief (<50-word) reasoning.

Behavior 1: A researcher finds a cluster of cancer cases in a neighborhood and assumes a local factory must be the cause, ignoring that random events naturally cluster.
Behavior 2: An investigator checks an unexpected result for coding errors but publishes a “significant” result that fits their theory without any additional verification.

text:

title: Statistical power | Week 04 In-class assignment
text:
Statistical power | Week 04 In-class assignment
This assignment is designed to help you build an intuitive, simulation-based understanding of statistical power — the probability that your test correctly detects a real effect when one exists.
The prewritten script implements a coin-flip experiment to detect biased coins, building up to a power curve that shows how detectable an effect is across a range of bias strengths:

Step 1 — Build the null distribution by simulating 10,000 fair-coin experiments
Step 2 — Run one experiment with a biased coin and compute a p-value
Step 3 — Estimate power by repeating the experiment 1,000 times
Step 4 — Sweep across all coin biases to generate a power curve

Here’s what to do:

Download the R script or Python script
Load it into your favorite IDE
Work with a partner to go through the code, discuss the logic in detail, and add detailed comments along the way
Run the code step-by-step and discuss the results at each stage
Answer the two questions at the end of the script

title: Bayesian | Week 07 Homework assignment
text:
Bayesian | Week 07 Homework assignment
Write your answers directly in text document, export as a PDF, and submit using this form.
Questions:

Complete the work you started in the class with relating the various descriptions and facts about the case of Jordan into probability and conditional probability statements following the Baye’s rule on Slide 10. Hint: If you pick ‘Jordan is a librarian’ as your hypothesis “H”, then “¬H” is ‘Jordan is a farmer’.


After you’ve worked out what each term is, connect the terms (and how they combine with each other) to how we worked out an answer simply from drawing boxes and writing down simple ratios.
Finally, calculate the answer based on the Baye’s rule equation and check against the answer you got before with Slide 4.


Make the table and do the calculations to answer the question about the practical utility of the “99% reliable” diagnostic test (Slide 13) in the following two scenarios: 1 in 10 have the disease and 1 in 1000 have the disease. Compare the answer to what you got in class when we assumed that 1 in 100 have the disease. Reflect on what these differences mean.
Work out the scenario in Slide 14 about the mammogram. What is the answer to the question? Are you provided with everything you need to answer it? Did you hvae to make assumptions to get to the answer and, if so, what?
Look at Slide 18 again (without looking at Slide 19) and try again to write FPR and FDR as conditional probabilities and interpret them intuitively. Then, connect your definitions and understanding back to Week 03 Slides 8 & 13, making the connection as explicit/concrete as possible.

title: P-values | Week 03 Homework assignment
text:
P-values | Week 03 Homework assignment
This assignment builds on the permutation test from the in-class activity and asks you to explore two additional ideas: how the properties of your data determine the p-value you get, and how p-hacking works in practice.
Download the R notebook or Python notebook, annotate the code, run it, and answer the questions embedded in the notebook as well as the questions below. Submit your completed notebook as a PDF using this form.


Reflecting on the in-class permutation test

In your own words, write a general definition of a p-value — one that is not tied to any specific test or dataset.
The permutation test and the Welch t-test gave similar p-values. Now suppose the data contained one very large outlier in group 2. Which method would you trust more, and why?




Part 2 — Effect of effect size, variance, and sample size

Look at the three-panel figure you produced. Describe the “boundary” between significant (red) and non-significant (blue) points. How does that boundary shift as you move from the left panel (SD = 0.5) to the right panel (SD = 2)?
A researcher runs a study and gets p = 0.06. They argue that with a larger sample size the result would surely become significant, and therefore the effect is real. Is this reasoning valid? Use what you observed in Part 2 to support your answer.




Part 3 — P-hacking

The simulation in Part 3 always finds p < 0.05 eventually, even though the data come from a single population with no real group difference. How many attempts did it take in your run? What does this tell you about the 0.05 threshold when testing is repeated?
Describe one real-world practice in data analysis or experimental design that is structurally equivalent to what the while-loop in Part 3 is doing. Explain the analogy clearly.

title: Variance, error, and uncertainty | Week 02 Homework assignment
text:
Variance, error, and uncertainty | Week 02 Homework assignment
This assignment is designed to help you to understand ways to estimate and report variation around an estimate.
Write your answers in a separate text docuent (include the figures you generate), convert to PDF, and submit using this form.

Which error bar is almost always the best to use around the mean? Explain.
Read the definition of the Central Limit Theorem from a textbook or Wikipedia, close the source, and write the definition in your own words. Then, point out the piece of code and the result in the in-class assignment that supports this theorem.
Explain the qualitative difference between Parts 1, 2, and 3 in the in-class assignment. What’s changing from 1-to-2 and 2-to-3 in terms of reflecting practical data collection and analysis scenarios?
Build on Part 3 of the in-class assignment to write code to calculate the 95% confidence interval. Write detailed documentation for each step to explain the logic.
Why do think the prediction interval is larger than the confidence interval. Explain in your own words. Next, how could we use bootstrapping to calculate the prediction interval?

title: Readings
text:
Checkout these resources after each lecture to solidify the discussion we had in class.

Week 01: Scientific method, Critical evaluation, and Cognitive biases

What is the question?: Mistaking the type of question being considered is the most common error in data analysis
How scientists fool themselves - and how they can stop: Seeking ways to fight our own worst instincts
P values are just the tip of the iceberg: Ridding science of shoddy statistics will require scrutiny of every step, not merely the last one



Week 02: Error and uncertainty estimation

Importance of being uncertain: Statistics does not tell us whether we are right. It tells us the chances of being wrong.
Error bars: The meaning of error bars is often misinterpreted, as is the statistical significance of their overlap.
Communicating uncertainty about facts, numbers and science



Week 03: P-values, P-hacking, and Multiple testing


Week 04: Error types and Statistical power


Week 05: Pseudoreplication and Confounding


Week 06: Circular analysis, Regression to the mean, and Sampling biases


Week 07: Bayesian reasoning


Week 08: Measuring correlations


Week 09: High-dimensional data and Machine learning pitfalls


Week 10: Data visualization and Statistical communication


Week 11: Reproducible research, Data sharing, and Ethical transparency


Week 12: AI-assisted data analysis

title: Preparatory checklists
text: Important note: This course assumes you have already learned basic statistical concepts in a traditional way. These preparation checklist contain Stats 101-level concepts you should be familiar with before each class. If you need to review any of these concepts, consult your introductory statistics textbook or freely available online resources before class.

title: Preparatory checklists
section: General prerequisites for the entire course
text:
General prerequisites for the entire course

Basic Statistics Knowledge

Descriptive statistics (mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation)
Probability basics
Hypothesis testing
Confidence intervals
Common statistical tests (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square)
Correlation and simple linear regression



Basic R or Python Skills

Reading and writing data files
Data manipulation (filtering, summarizing)
Basic plotting
Running statistical tests
Installing packages/libraries

title: Preparatory checklists
text:
How to use this guide

Before each class: Scan the concepts listed for that week
Self-check: Can you define each concept in one sentence?
Quick review: If anything is unfamiliar, do a brief refresher
Come prepared: Solid foundations will help us effectively engage in discussing reinterpretations and nuances


Week 01: Scientific method, Critical evaluation, and Cognitive biases

Scientific method

Null and alternative hypotheses

Experimental vs. observational studies

Randomized controlled trials (RCTs)

Cohort studies

Case-control studies

Cross-sectional studies



Week 02: Error and uncertainty estimation

Normal distribution

Standard deviation vs. standard error

Confidence intervals

Point estimates

Sampling distributions

Central Limit Theorem



Week 03: P-values, P-hacking, and Multiple testing

P-values

Statistical significance (α = 0.05)

Null hypothesis significance testing

Type I error (false positive)

Type II error (false negative)



Week 04: Error types and Statistical power

Statistical power

Type I and Type II errors

Effect sizes (Cohen’s d, r)

Sample size

Alpha (α) and beta (β)



Week 05: Pseudoreplication and Confounding

Sample size (n)

Independent observations

Correlation

Confounding variables



Week 06: Circular analysis, Regression to the mean, and Sampling biases

Training vs. test data

Random sampling

Selection bias

Measurement error



Week 07: Bayesian reasoning

Conditional probability

Bayes’ theorem

Sensitivity and specificity

Positive and negative predictive values

Prevalence



Week 08: Measuring correlations

Pearson correlation

Scatterplots

Linear regression

R-squared

Residuals



Week 09: High-dimensional data and Machine learning pitfalls

Overfitting

Training and test sets

Cross-validation

Model fitting



Week 10: Data visualization and Statistical communication

Bar charts

Scatterplots

Histograms

Box plots

Axes and scales



Week 11: Reproducible research, Data sharing, and Ethical transparency

Research documentation

Data organization

File management

Research ethics basics



Week 12: AI-assisted data analysis
No specific prerequisites; this is a new topic. Just come with an open mind and, if possible, some experience using AI tools like ChatGPT or Claude for coding or analysis.

title: Pseudoreplication and Confounding | Week 05 Homework assignment
text:
Pseudoreplication and Confounding | Week 05 Homework assignment
Write your answers directly in text document, export as a PDF, and submit using this form.
Questions:

You are reviewing a mouse study where pups from the same litter are treated as independent replicates. The paper reports n=30 (pups) but there are only 5 litters. What specific statistical problem does this create, and what would you recommend the authors do instead?
In the Blainey et al. framework, biological variance is usually much larger than technical variance. If that’s true, why would anyone ever invest in technical replicates? Under what experimental circumstances does it actually make sense to prioritize technical over biological replication?
The Gilad & Mizrahi-Man reanalysis showed that correcting for batch in the mouse ENCODE data reversed the main biological conclusion. But one reviewer pointed out that if batch and species are nearly perfectly confounded, applying ComBat effectively removes the species signal too — making it impossible to recover the true answer from this dataset. Does that mean Gilad’s correction was wrong? What does this situation reveal about the limits of post-hoc batch correction vs. study design?
Leek et al. argue that normalization does not remove batch effects and can even exacerbate them. Why? What is it about the nature of batch effects that makes standard normalization insufficient?

title: Variance, error, and uncertainty | Week 02 In-class assignment
text:
Variance, error, and uncertainty | Week 02 In-class assignment
This assignment is designed to help you to execute and understand three ways to estimate the standard error of the mean.
This prewritten script illustrates three approaches to the standard error of the mean:

Part 1 — Formula-based SEM from a single sample
Part 2 — Empirical SEM via repeated sampling from a known population
Part 3 — Bootstrap SEM from a single sample (no population needed)

Here’s what to do:

Download the R script or Python script
Load it into your favorite IDE
Work with a partner to go through the code, discuss the logic in detail, and add detailed comments along the way
Finally, run the code part-by-part and discuss the results.

title: Statistical power | Week 04 Homework assignment
text:
Statistical power | Week 04 Homework assignment
This assignment extends the in-class power analysis to multiple sample sizes, so you can see how power depends on both effect size and the number of observations you collect.
Write your answers directly in the script as comments, export as a PDF, and submit using this form.

Download the R script or Python script
Add detailed comments at every step explaining both what the code does and what it means statistically
Run the code and answer all questions in the spaces provided

Questions:

What are your observations on how the null distribution changes with sample size?
What are your interpretations of the multiple power curves? Write your thoughts in terms of the dependence of power on both effect size and sample size.
A national sports organisation wants to design an experiment to detect biased coins used in pre-game tosses. How would you use the power curves above to help them design the experiment? What questions would you need answered before making a specific sample-size recommendation?
If you make a specific sample-size recommendation, write down what you will convey to the organisation in terms of error rates (false-positive rate and false-negative rate).
Which parts of your reasoning and recommendations change if the organisation says it cannot tolerate more than 1 biased coin for every 100 coins it uses? (Hint: this corresponds to changing alpha from 0.05 to 0.01.)

title: Correlation coefficients | Week 08 Homework assignment
text:
Correlation coefficients | Week 08 Homework assignment
This assignment extends the in-class simulation to ask how sample size shapes the null distribution of Pearson r and what that means for interpreting reported correlations.
Download the R script or Python script, annotate the code, run it, and answer the questions below. Write your answers directly in the script as comments, export as a PDF, and submit using this form.


Describe how the shape of the null distribution changes as n increases. Comment on both the width (spread) and the symmetry across the five panels.
Report the critical r at n = 5 and n = 100. What does the difference imply for interpreting r = 0.35 from a study with n = 20 versus r = 0.10 from a study with n = 500? Which finding is more likely to be “statistically significant”? Which is more likely to matter practically?
With very large n (e.g., n = 10,000), virtually any non-zero r would be “statistically significant.” What should a researcher report alongside a p-value to convey whether a significant correlation is practically meaningful?
The dashed theory curve (1.645 / √(n − 1)) tracks the simulation well at large n but diverges slightly at n = 5. Why might the approximation be less accurate at very small sample sizes?
Extend the code in Step 2 to also compute the 2.5th percentile (the lower-tail critical value). Add both the upper and lower critical values as vertical lines in the Step 3 plot. What is the two-tailed critical |r| at n = 10, and how does it compare to the one-tailed value?

title: Circular Analysis and Sampling Bias | Week 06 Homework assignment
text:
Circular Analysis and Sampling Bias | Week 06 Homework assignment
This assignment extends the in-class introduction to circular analysis (double-dipping) and sampling bias. You will work through four coding exercises that each isolate a distinct mechanism by which an analysis pipeline can produce misleading results even when no real effect exists: clustering and testing on the same data, inheriting inflated group structure through label propagation, labeling by proximity to arbitrary reference points, and comparing extreme values across populations of unequal size.
Download the R script or Python script, annotate the code, run it, and answer the questions embedded in the script as well as the questions below. Submit your completed script as a PDF using this form.


Exercise 1 — Circular analysis: cluster-then-test on the same data

Look at the p-values and effect sizes your script produced. Are they what you would expect if the null hypothesis (no group differences) were true? What do these results tell you — and what do they not tell you — about the data?
K-means minimises within-cluster sum of squares. Explain in your own words why that objective makes the subsequent t-test circular. What would a valid test of group differences require that this pipeline does not provide?




Exercise 2 — Circular analysis: a partial fix via data splitting

Compare the p-values from Exercise 2 (data splitting) with those from Exercise 1 (no split). Are they qualitatively different? Does data splitting appear to eliminate significance inflation? What does this suggest about treating data splitting as a universal safeguard against circular analysis?
The k-NN classifier assigns test-set labels by spatial proximity to training points. In what sense is this still a form of double-dipping, even though the exact same rows are not reused? What property of a labeling rule makes a subsequent hypothesis test circular, regardless of whether the data are split?




Exercise 3 — Circular analysis: the Voronoi / proximity-labeling variant

The Voronoi boundary in Exercise 3 is the y-axis (the perpendicular bisector of segment AB). How does the orientation of AB relative to the tested variable x affect the magnitude of the resulting p-value? Predict what would happen — and why — if you rotated AB to lie along the y-axis instead (e.g., ref_A = (0, −1), ref_B = (0, 1)). Test your prediction by modifying the script.
In Exercise 2, labels were propagated by k-NN proximity. In Exercise 3, labels are assigned by proximity to two fixed reference points. Identify the shared flaw in these two procedures. State in general terms: what property of a labeling rule makes a subsequent hypothesis test circular?




Exercise 4 — Sampling bias: outliers and population size

The log-normal curve starts lower than both the normal and exponential curves at small population sizes. Explain why, in terms of the tail behaviour of each distribution. Your answer should reference the approximate growth formulas discussed in the script comments (√(2 ln n) for the normal, ln n for the exponential, exp(σ √(2 ln n)) for the log-normal).
World athletics records are maintained separately for each age group (under-20, open, masters). Suppose a journalist reports that a 35-year-old holds a better marathon record than any under-20 athlete ever has. Using the intuition from Exercise 4, what question would you ask before accepting that conclusion, and what additional analysis would you propose?
Rare genetic variants are far more likely to be discovered in large biobanks (n ~ 500,000) than in small cohort studies (n ~ 500). How does the sampling-bias argument from Exercise 4 apply to interpreting effect-size estimates for variants initially discovered in large vs. small studies? (Hint: think about which variants a small study can detect and how that selection shapes the distribution of reported effect sizes.)

title: P-values | Week 03 In-class assignment
text:
P-values | Week 03 In-class assignment
This assignment is designed to help you build a hands-on, ground-up understanding of p-values by implementing a permutation test from scratch — computing a p-value without invoking any formula or distributional assumption.
The notebook walks through a step-by-step permutation test on a simulated clinical trial (placebo vs. drug), and ends by comparing the result to what a standard t-test gives.
Here’s what to do:

Download the R notebook or Python notebook
Work with a partner to go through the code step by step — for each block, discuss what it’s doing before moving on
Add detailed comments at every step explaining both what the code does and what it means statistically — annotate as if writing for someone who has never heard of a permutation test
Run the code block by block and talk through the output at each stage
Answer the two questions at the end of the notebook

title: Recommended Preparatory Materials
text:
Everything recommended here is free and open.




Khan Academy – Statistics
Modern Statistics for Modern Biology (book + code + solutions) – This book is really good!





Beginner-friendly installation instructions for R and RStudio
An opinionated tour of RStudio for new users
A step-by-step guide to installing and using R packages
RStudio Primers – Perfect for beginner – I recommend covering the following 6 primers.

The Basics
Work with Data
Visualize Data
Tidy Your Data
Iterate
Write Functions

A gentle introduction to tidy statistics in R

This one-hour introduction covers how to get started quickly with the basics of research statistics in R, providing an emphasis on reading data into R, exploratory data analysis with the tidyverse, statistical testing with ANOVAs, and finally producing a publication-ready plot in ggplot2.

R for Data Science (An awesome book on using R for data analysis and visualization!)
R Cheatsheets (Great set of visually-appealing cheatsheets)





Introduction to Python
Jupyter Notebooks
Data wrangling
Data cleaning
Data visualization
Python Plotting for Exploratory Data Analysis
Statistics in Python





Command-line refresher from Software Carpentry
Command-line Bootcamp
Command-line Guide | Also interactive, just like the bootcamp.
